1:
"The priests the Levites, and all the tribe of Levi, shall have no part nor inheritance with Israel: they shall eat the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and his inheritance." The witnesses note the priests and Levites having no land-inheritance, but eating the offerings of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had no part in the division of Canaan among the tribes. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them eating the offerings of the LORD made by fire as their portion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no part with Israel — no land in Canaan; the eat the offerings — fed from the altar; the his inheritance — the LORD's own portion shared; the they that serve the altar — live of the altar (1 Cor. 9:13-14).

The witnesses agree the want of a land-portion was supplied by a better one — the LORD's own offerings: the priests and Levites had no field in Canaan, but ate "the offerings of the LORD made by fire," fed from his altar; the arrangement bound the ministers wholly to God's service and to God's provision, that they who served the altar should live of the altar; the apostle takes up this very order — "they which minister about holy things live of the things of the temple... even so hath the Lord ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel" (1 Cor. 9:13-14).

So the witnesses make the verse no inheritance but the offerings: The witnesses note the no part with Israel: no land in Canaan. The witnesses note the eat the offerings: fed from the altar. The witnesses note the his inheritance: the LORD's own portion shared. The witnesses note the they that serve the altar: live of the altar (1 Cor. 9:13-14). The expositors observe the no part with Israel: no land in Canaan. The expositors observe the eat the offerings: fed from the altar. The expositors observe the his inheritance: the LORD's own portion shared. The expositors observe the they that serve the altar: live of the altar (1 Cor. 9:13-14). The believer is taught to mark the no part with Israel, the eat the offerings, the his inheritance, the they that serve the altar, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the Levites had no land but ate the offerings of the LORD — bound wholly to his service and provision, they who serve the altar living of the altar (1 Cor. 9:13-14).
2:
"Therefore shall they have no inheritance among their brethren: the LORD is their inheritance, as he hath said unto them." The witnesses note again that the Levites had no inheritance, for the LORD himself was their portion. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had neither field nor vineyard, provision being made for them otherwise. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD as their inheritance, as he had said. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no inheritance — no land among the brethren; the the LORD is their inheritance — god himself their portion; the as he hath said — his own appointment; the the believer's confession — the LORD my portion (Lam. 3:24).

The witnesses agree the repeated word lifts the Levites' privilege to its height: lacking land among their brethren, "the LORD is their inheritance" — God himself their portion in place of fields; the exchange marks the dignity of those given wholly to his service, that they find their all in him; and it is the believer's own confession, who can say "the LORD is my portion... therefore will I hope in him" (Lam. 3:24), counting God a better inheritance than any earthly possession, the soul satisfied in him alone.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD their portion: The witnesses note the no inheritance: no land among the brethren. The witnesses note the the LORD is their inheritance: God himself their portion. The witnesses note the as he hath said: his own appointment. The witnesses note the the believer's confession: the LORD my portion (Lam. 3:24). The expositors observe the no inheritance: no land among the brethren. The expositors observe the the LORD is their inheritance: God himself their portion. The expositors observe the as he hath said: his own appointment. The expositors observe the the believer's confession: the LORD my portion (Lam. 3:24). The believer is taught to mark the no inheritance, the the LORD is their inheritance, the as he hath said, the the believer's confession, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the Levites had no land, for the LORD was their inheritance — God himself the portion of his servants, the believer's own confession that the LORD is my portion (Lam. 3:24).
3:
"And this shall be the priest's due from the people... they shall give unto the priest the shoulder, and the two cheeks, and the maw." The witnesses note the priest's portion from the people's sacrifices — the shoulder, cheeks, and maw. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was due not from the priests but from those who brought the sacrifices, particularly the peace offerings. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the word zebach is used of sacrifices. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priest's due — from the people's sacrifices; the the shoulder, cheeks, maw — his appointed portion; the of the peace offerings — a fixed right; the the ministry maintained — by the offerings of the people (Gal. 6:6).

The witnesses agree the appointed portions secured the priest's maintenance from the people's offerings: of the peace offerings the priest received the shoulder, cheeks, and maw, a fixed due that none might withhold; the rule shows God's care to provide for his ministers by a settled right, not by the people's changing goodwill, and binds the worshipper to support those who serve at the altar; the giving of a portion of the sacrifice to the priest teaches that they who bring their gifts to God are to provide also for his servants, the ministry maintained by the offerings of the people (cf. Gal. 6:6).

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's due: The witnesses note the the priest's due: from the people's sacrifices. The witnesses note the the shoulder, cheeks, maw: his appointed portion. The witnesses note the of the peace offerings: a fixed right. The witnesses note the the ministry maintained: by the offerings of the people (Gal. 6:6). The expositors observe the the priest's due: from the people's sacrifices. The expositors observe the the shoulder, cheeks, maw: his appointed portion. The expositors observe the of the peace offerings: a fixed right. The expositors observe the the ministry maintained: by the offerings of the people (Gal. 6:6). The believer is taught to mark the the priest's due, the the shoulder, cheeks, maw, the of the peace offerings, the the ministry maintained, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the priest received the shoulder, cheeks, and maw of the offerings — God providing for his ministers by a settled right, the ministry maintained by the gifts of the people (Gal. 6:6).
4:
"The firstfruit also of thy corn, of thy wine, and of thine oil, and the first of the fleece of thy sheep, shalt thou give him." The witnesses note the firstfruits of corn, wine, oil, and fleece given to the priest. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the heave offering, the offering of the firstfruits, the measure fixed by the Jews. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the firstfruit of corn, wine, oil, and the first fleece. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the firstfruit of corn, wine, oil — the best of the increase; the the first fleece — of the sheep; the given to the priest — the first portion God's due; the Christ the firstfruits — the first and best offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20).

The witnesses agree the firstfruits given to the priest acknowledged God as the giver of the harvest: the first of the corn, wine, oil, and fleece was rendered to his minister, the best and earliest devoted to him; the giving of the firstfruits confessed that the whole increase came from God and that the first portion was his due, the people honouring the LORD "with the firstfruits of all thine increase" (Prov. 3:9); and the firstfruits throughout point to Christ, "the firstfruits of them that slept" (1 Cor. 15:20), the first and best offered to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the firstfruits: The witnesses note the firstfruit of corn, wine, oil: the best of the increase. The witnesses note the the first fleece: of the sheep. The witnesses note the given to the priest: the first portion God's due. The witnesses note the Christ the firstfruits: the first and best offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20). The expositors observe the firstfruit of corn, wine, oil: the best of the increase. The expositors observe the the first fleece: of the sheep. The expositors observe the given to the priest: the first portion God's due. The expositors observe the Christ the firstfruits: the first and best offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20). The believer is taught to mark the firstfruit of corn, wine, oil, the the first fleece, the given to the priest, the Christ the firstfruits, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the firstfruits of corn, wine, oil, and fleece were given to the priest — confessing God the giver of all, the first and best his due, pointing to Christ the firstfruits (Prov. 3:9; 1 Cor. 15:20).
5:
"For the LORD thy God hath chosen him out of all thy tribes, to stand to minister in the name of the LORD, him and his sons for ever." The witnesses note Levi chosen out of all the tribes to minister in the LORD's name for ever. The witnesses note that Gill notes God chose the tribe of Levi out of all the other tribes of Israel. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the reason of the priests' right — chosen to stand and minister in the name of the LORD, he and his sons for ever. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the chosen out of all tribes — the divine election; the to stand to minister — the sacred office; the in the name of the LORD — god's representative; the him and his sons for ever — pointing to the eternal High Priest (Heb. 7:24).

The witnesses agree the maintenance of the Levites rested on their divine election to ministry: God "hath chosen him out of all thy tribes, to stand to minister in the name of the LORD," a perpetual office for them and their sons; the choosing of one tribe for the sacred service marks that the ministry is a divine calling, not a human appointment, and that those whom God sets apart to serve him are to be honoured and provided for; the standing to minister "in the name of the LORD" shows the priest as God's representative to the people, pointing to the great High Priest who ministers in his Father's name for ever (cf. Heb. 7:24).

So the witnesses make the verse chosen to minister: The witnesses note the chosen out of all tribes: the divine election. The witnesses note the to stand to minister: the sacred office. The witnesses note the in the name of the LORD: God's representative. The witnesses note the him and his sons for ever: pointing to the eternal High Priest (Heb. 7:24). The expositors observe the chosen out of all tribes: the divine election. The expositors observe the to stand to minister: the sacred office. The expositors observe the in the name of the LORD: God's representative. The expositors observe the him and his sons for ever: pointing to the eternal High Priest (Heb. 7:24). The believer is taught to mark the chosen out of all tribes, the to stand to minister, the in the name of the LORD, the him and his sons for ever, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God chose Levi to minister in his name for ever — the ministry a divine calling, the priest God's representative, pointing to the great High Priest who ministers for ever (Heb. 7:24).
6:
"And if a Levite come from any of thy gates out of all Israel, where he sojourned, and come with all the desire of his mind unto the place which the LORD shall choose;" The witnesses note the Levite from any city free to come to the chosen place to minister, with all his heart's desire. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Levites were dispersed all over the country, employed in teaching the people, and were but sojourners except in their own cities. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Levite coming with all the desire of his mind. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a Levite from any city — dispersed and sojourning; the come to the chosen place — free to minister there; the all the desire of his mind — a whole-hearted longing; the the spirit God loves — willing service, not grudging (Ps. 84:10).

The witnesses agree the verse honours the Levite's zeal and grants him liberty to serve: though dispersed through the land as a sojourner and teacher, any Levite might come "with all the desire of his mind" to the chosen place and there minister; the welcome of the willing servant shows that God values the heart's desire in his service, opening the way for those who long to draw near and minister; the phrase "all the desire of his mind" marks the spirit God loves in his servants — not a grudging duty but a whole-hearted longing to serve at his altar (cf. Ps. 84:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the willing Levite: The witnesses note the a Levite from any city: dispersed and sojourning. The witnesses note the come to the chosen place: free to minister there. The witnesses note the all the desire of his mind: a whole-hearted longing. The witnesses note the the spirit God loves: willing service, not grudging (Ps. 84:10). The expositors observe the a Levite from any city: dispersed and sojourning. The expositors observe the come to the chosen place: free to minister there. The expositors observe the all the desire of his mind: a whole-hearted longing. The expositors observe the the spirit God loves: willing service, not grudging (Ps. 84:10). The believer is taught to mark the a Levite from any city, the come to the chosen place, the all the desire of his mind, the the spirit God loves, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that any Levite might come to the chosen place to minister with all his heart's desire — God honouring the willing servant's zeal, the whole-hearted longing to serve at his altar the spirit he loves (Ps. 84:10).
7:
"Then he shall minister in the name of the LORD his God, as all his brethren the Levites do, which stand there before the LORD." The witnesses note the visiting Levite allowed to minister in the LORD's name, as his brethren who served there. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Targum reads "minister in the name of the Word of the Lord" — in the name of Christ, as a type of him, as every priest and sacrifice were; he might officiate though it was not his course. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him ministering as his brethren the Levites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he shall minister — the willing Levite admitted; the in the name of the LORD — as a type of Christ; the as his brethren do — serving at the altar; the the one true Priest — in whose name all ministry is done (Heb. 9:24).

The witnesses agree the Levite's ministry was service "in the name of the LORD," and a type of Christ: even out of his appointed course, the willing Levite might serve at the altar as his brethren did; Gill notes the Targum's reading — to minister "in the name of the Word of the Lord," that is, of Christ, every priest being a type of him; the whole priestly service thus pointed beyond itself to the one true Priest, in whose name all acceptable ministry is done, the Levites' standing before the LORD a shadow of the Mediator who stands for his people (cf. Heb. 9:24).

So the witnesses make the verse minister in the LORD's name: The witnesses note the he shall minister: the willing Levite admitted. The witnesses note the in the name of the LORD: as a type of Christ. The witnesses note the as his brethren do: serving at the altar. The witnesses note the the one true Priest: in whose name all ministry is done (Heb. 9:24). The expositors observe the he shall minister: the willing Levite admitted. The expositors observe the in the name of the LORD: as a type of Christ. The expositors observe the as his brethren do: serving at the altar. The expositors observe the the one true Priest: in whose name all ministry is done (Heb. 9:24). The believer is taught to mark the he shall minister, the in the name of the LORD, the as his brethren do, the the one true Priest, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the Levite ministered in the name of the LORD, as his brethren — the priestly service a type of Christ, every priest pointing to the one true Priest in whose name all acceptable ministry is done (Heb. 9:24).
8:
"They shall have like portions to eat, beside that which cometh of the sale of his patrimony." The witnesses note the visiting Levite given an equal portion of the offerings with the resident priests. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had equal parts of the sacrifices with the priests who usually ministered there; Jarchi infers they divided the skins and flesh of the sin offerings. The witnesses note that this was beside what came of the sale of his patrimony. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the like portions to eat — equal shares of the offerings; the with the resident priests — no partiality; the beside his patrimony — besides his own means; the brotherly fairness — all sharing alike in the work (1 Cor. 9:14).

The witnesses agree the equal portion marks a fair and brotherly sharing among the ministers: the visiting Levite was to "have like portions" with those who usually served, an equal share of the offerings, besides his own private means; the rule guards against grudging or partiality among God's servants, that the willing comer be not slighted but received as an equal in the sacred work; it teaches the spirit of fairness and fellowship that should reign among those who serve God together, none lording it over another, but all sharing alike in the labour and its provision (cf. 1 Cor. 9:14).

So the witnesses make the verse like portions: The witnesses note the like portions to eat: equal shares of the offerings. The witnesses note the with the resident priests: no partiality. The witnesses note the beside his patrimony: besides his own means. The witnesses note the brotherly fairness: all sharing alike in the work (1 Cor. 9:14). The expositors observe the like portions to eat: equal shares of the offerings. The expositors observe the with the resident priests: no partiality. The expositors observe the beside his patrimony: besides his own means. The expositors observe the brotherly fairness: all sharing alike in the work (1 Cor. 9:14). The believer is taught to mark the like portions to eat, the with the resident priests, the beside his patrimony, the brotherly fairness, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the visiting Levite had an equal portion with the resident priests — a fair and brotherly sharing among God's servants, the willing comer received as an equal in the sacred work (1 Cor. 9:14).
9:
"When thou art come into the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee, thou shalt not learn to do after the abominations of those nations." The witnesses note the charge, on entering the land, not to learn the abominable practices of its nations. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Canaan is often thus described to express God's goodness in bestowing it as a mere favour, typical of the heavenly Canaan, the free gift of God through Christ. The witnesses note that they were not to learn the abominations of the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come into the land — the free gift of grace; the learn not to do — no imitating the heathen; the the abominations — the nations' wickedness; the holy separation — be not conformed to the world (Rom. 12:2).

The witnesses agree the entrance into the good land carried a solemn warning against its corruptions: Israel was "not to learn to do after the abominations" of the nations they dispossessed, lest the land's former sins become their own; the danger of imitating the wickedness around them was real, and the charge presses a holy separation from the practices of the heathen; Gill marks the land itself a free gift of grace, type of the heavenly inheritance through Christ — and as the earthly Canaan was to be kept pure, so the people of God are called to holiness, "be not conformed to this world" (Rom. 12:2).

So the witnesses make the verse learn not their abominations: The witnesses note the come into the land: the free gift of grace. The witnesses note the learn not to do: no imitating the heathen. The witnesses note the the abominations: the nations' wickedness. The witnesses note the holy separation: be not conformed to the world (Rom. 12:2). The expositors observe the come into the land: the free gift of grace. The expositors observe the learn not to do: no imitating the heathen. The expositors observe the the abominations: the nations' wickedness. The expositors observe the holy separation: be not conformed to the world (Rom. 12:2). The believer is taught to mark the come into the land, the learn not to do, the the abominations, the holy separation, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was not to learn the abominations of the nations — a holy separation from heathen wickedness, the people of God called not to be conformed to the world (Rom. 12:2).
10:
"There shall not be found among you any one that maketh his son or his daughter to pass through the fire, or that useth divination, or an observer of times, or an enchanter, or a witch," The witnesses note the forbidding of child-sacrifice to Molech, divination, and the dark arts. The witnesses note that Gill notes the passing through fire was the service of Molech — the child led by a priest between two fires, a devoting of him to that deity. The witnesses note that divination, observing of times, enchanting, and witchcraft were all forbidden. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass through the fire — the worship of Molech; the divination — observing of times; the enchanter, witch — the dark arts; the no traffic with darkness — seeking God alone (Eph. 5:11).

The witnesses agree the verse heaps up the abominations God utterly forbids — the worship of Molech and the whole train of occult arts: to make a child "pass through the fire" to Molech, to practise divination, soothsaying, enchantment, or witchcraft, were all banned as the dark religion of the heathen; the law sweeps away every means by which men sought hidden knowledge or power apart from God, for all such traffic with darkness is rebellion against the LORD who alone reveals his will; the people of God are to seek him only, and to have no fellowship with "the unfruitful works of darkness" (Eph. 5:11).

So the witnesses make the verse the abominations forbidden: The witnesses note the pass through the fire: the worship of Molech. The witnesses note the divination: observing of times. The witnesses note the enchanter, witch: the dark arts. The witnesses note the no traffic with darkness: seeking God alone (Eph. 5:11). The expositors observe the pass through the fire: the worship of Molech. The expositors observe the divination: observing of times. The expositors observe the enchanter, witch: the dark arts. The expositors observe the no traffic with darkness: seeking God alone (Eph. 5:11). The believer is taught to mark the pass through the fire, the divination, the enchanter, witch, the no traffic with darkness, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that child-sacrifice to Molech, divination, and witchcraft were utterly forbidden — every occult means of knowledge or power banned, the people of God to seek him alone and have no fellowship with darkness (Eph. 5:11).
11:
"Or a charmer, or a consulter with familiar spirits, or a wizard, or a necromancer." The witnesses note the further forbidden arts — charming, consulting familiar spirits, wizardry, and necromancy. The witnesses note that Gill notes the charmer, by the Targums, binds serpents and scorpions, or says certain words; the consulter with familiar spirits and the necromancer sought the dead. The witnesses note that all these were forbidden. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a charmer — binding by spells; the consulter with familiar spirits — the medium; the a wizard — the dark knowledge; the a necromancer — seeking the dead (Isa. 8:19).

The witnesses agree the catalogue is completed with the darkest of the arts — the consulting of spirits and the seeking of the dead: the charmer, the medium with a "familiar spirit," the wizard, and the necromancer who would inquire of the dead, were all utterly forbidden; the seeking of secret knowledge from spirits or the departed is a turning from the living God to the powers of darkness, a thing he abhors; the law would have his people inquire of him alone and not of the dead, "should not a people seek unto their God? for the living to the dead?" (Isa. 8:19), all such commerce with spirits being rebellion against the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse spirits and the dead: The witnesses note the a charmer: binding by spells. The witnesses note the consulter with familiar spirits: the medium. The witnesses note the a wizard: the dark knowledge. The witnesses note the a necromancer: seeking the dead (Isa. 8:19). The expositors observe the a charmer: binding by spells. The expositors observe the consulter with familiar spirits: the medium. The expositors observe the a wizard: the dark knowledge. The expositors observe the a necromancer: seeking the dead (Isa. 8:19). The believer is taught to mark the a charmer, the consulter with familiar spirits, the a wizard, the a necromancer, and to be perfect with the LORD his God. The verse thus records that charming, consulting spirits, wizardry, and necromancy were forbidden — the seeking of secret knowledge from spirits or the dead a turning from the living God, his people to inquire of him alone (Isa. 8:19).
12:
"For all that do these things are an abomination unto the LORD: and because of these abominations the LORD thy God doth drive them out from before thee." The witnesses note that all who practise these dark arts are an abomination, for which God drives out the nations. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes it is not all these things together but whoever does any of them, and likewise all who consult them; especially abominable in Israel to encourage such. The witnesses note that for these abominations God was driving out the nations. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all that do these things — any of the dark arts; the an abomination — god's abhorrence of the occult; the drive them out — the nations doomed for it; the the same sin, same judgment — the land kept pure (Lev. 18:24-25).

The witnesses agree the verse declares both God's abhorrence of the occult and the reason for the nations' doom: "all that do these things are an abomination," and it was for these very abominations that God was casting the nations out of the land; the canon Gill draws — that any one of these arts, or the consulting of those who use them, defiles — shows how God hates all traffic with darkness; and the casting-out of the Canaanites for it warns Israel that the same sins would bring the same judgment, the holiness of the land requiring its purity from every occult abomination (cf. Lev. 18:24-25).

So the witnesses make the verse an abomination: The witnesses note the all that do these things: any of the dark arts. The witnesses note the an abomination: God's abhorrence of the occult. The witnesses note the drive them out: the nations doomed for it. The witnesses note the the same sin, same judgment: the land kept pure (Lev. 18:24-25). The expositors observe the all that do these things: any of the dark arts. The expositors observe the an abomination: God's abhorrence of the occult. The expositors observe the drive them out: the nations doomed for it. The expositors observe the the same sin, same judgment: the land kept pure (Lev. 18:24-25). The believer is taught to mark the all that do these things, the an abomination, the drive them out, the the same sin, same judgment, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that all who practise these arts are an abomination, for which God drove out the nations — his abhorrence of all traffic with darkness, the same sin warned to bring the same judgment (Lev. 18:24-25).
13:
"Thou shalt be perfect with the LORD thy God." The witnesses note the positive charge over against the occult — to be perfect, blameless, with the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes this means sincerely to serve and worship him, faithfully adhere to his word and ordinances, and walk uprightly before him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Israel to be blameless in its intercourse with Jehovah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the perfect with the LORD — sincere and upright; the over against the occult — no dark arts; the cleaving wholly to him — an undivided integrity; the walk before God blameless — as Abraham was charged (Gen. 17:1).

The witnesses agree the brief charge sets the whole duty over against the foregoing abominations: instead of seeking hidden things by dark arts, Israel was to be "perfect" — sincere, upright, and blameless — in their walk with God, cleaving wholly to him and his word; the perfection meant is not sinlessness but a single-hearted integrity, an undivided cleaving to the LORD alone; it is the same call given to Abraham, "walk before me, and be thou perfect" (Gen. 17:1), that the people of God seek and trust him only, with a whole and upright heart.

So the witnesses make the verse be perfect: The witnesses note the perfect with the LORD: sincere and upright. The witnesses note the over against the occult: no dark arts. The witnesses note the cleaving wholly to him: an undivided integrity. The witnesses note the walk before God blameless: as Abraham was charged (Gen. 17:1). The expositors observe the perfect with the LORD: sincere and upright. The expositors observe the over against the occult: no dark arts. The expositors observe the cleaving wholly to him: an undivided integrity. The expositors observe the walk before God blameless: as Abraham was charged (Gen. 17:1). The believer is taught to mark the perfect with the LORD, the over against the occult, the cleaving wholly to him, the walk before God blameless, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that Israel was to be perfect with the LORD — a single-hearted, upright integrity over against the occult, the people of God cleaving wholly to him, walking before him blameless (Gen. 17:1).
14:
"For these nations, which thou shalt possess, hearkened unto observers of times, and unto diviners: but as for thee, the LORD thy God hath not suffered thee so to do." The witnesses note the contrast — the nations sought diviners, but God had not suffered Israel to do so. The witnesses note that Gill notes those nations hearkened to observers of times and diviners and did as they directed. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the contrast — but God had not suffered Israel so to do. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the nations hearkened — to observers and diviners; the groping after the future — by forbidden means; the but as for thee — god had not suffered Israel so; the the privilege of revelation — god making known his will (prepares v15).

The witnesses agree the verse draws a sharp line between Israel and the nations in the matter of guidance: the heathen "hearkened unto observers of times and diviners," groping after the future by dark and forbidden means, "but as for thee, the LORD thy God hath not suffered thee so to do"; the difference is the privilege of revelation — Israel needed no diviners, for God himself made known his will to them by his prophets; the contrast prepares for the promise that follows, that God would supply, in the true Prophet, all and more than the nations vainly sought from their soothsayers.

So the witnesses make the verse not suffered so: The witnesses note the the nations hearkened: to observers and diviners. The witnesses note the groping after the future: by forbidden means. The witnesses note the but as for thee: God had not suffered Israel so. The witnesses note the the privilege of revelation: God making known his will (prepares v15). The expositors observe the the nations hearkened: to observers and diviners. The expositors observe the groping after the future: by forbidden means. The expositors observe the but as for thee: God had not suffered Israel so. The expositors observe the the privilege of revelation: God making known his will (prepares v15). The believer is taught to mark the the nations hearkened, the groping after the future, the but as for thee, the the privilege of revelation, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that the nations sought diviners, but God had not suffered Israel so to do — the privilege of revelation distinguishing them, God himself making known his will, preparing for the promise of the Prophet.
15:
"The LORD thy God will raise up unto thee a Prophet from the midst of thee, of thy brethren, like unto me; unto him ye shall hearken;" The witnesses note the great promise of the Prophet like Moses, whom God would raise up and to whom they must hearken. The witnesses note that Gill insists this is not Joshua, Jeremiah, David, nor a succession of prophets, for a single person is spoken of and there is dissimilitude between Moses and any other; but the Messiah, with whom the whole agrees. The witnesses note that unto him they were to hearken. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the will raise up a Prophet — god's great promise; the of thy brethren, like unto me — one like Moses; the a single person — not the prophets, but the Messiah; the unto him ye shall hearken — christ the great Prophet (Acts 3:22).

The witnesses agree this is one of the great Messianic promises of the law: God would raise up "a Prophet... like unto me," to whom all must hearken; Gill shows it cannot be Joshua, or the prophets, or any succession, for "a single person is only spoken of" and none of them was truly "like unto Moses" — but the Messiah, the one Prophet who, like Moses, was mediator, lawgiver, and worker of mighty signs; the apostles claimed its fulfilment in Christ (Acts 3:22; John 6:14), the great Prophet to whom God commands his people to hearken, the final and perfect revealer of his will.

So the witnesses make the verse the Prophet like Moses: The witnesses note the will raise up a Prophet: God's great promise. The witnesses note the of thy brethren, like unto me: one like Moses. The witnesses note the a single person: not the prophets, but the Messiah. The witnesses note the unto him ye shall hearken: Christ the great Prophet (Acts 3:22). The expositors observe the will raise up a Prophet: God's great promise. The expositors observe the of thy brethren, like unto me: one like Moses. The expositors observe the a single person: not the prophets, but the Messiah. The expositors observe the unto him ye shall hearken: Christ the great Prophet (Acts 3:22). The believer is taught to mark the will raise up a Prophet, the of thy brethren, like unto me, the a single person, the unto him ye shall hearken, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that God promised a Prophet like Moses, to whom they must hearken — fulfilled not in any prophet but in the Messiah, Christ the great Prophet, the final revealer of God's will (Acts 3:22; John 6:14).
16:
"According to all that thou desiredst of the LORD thy God in Horeb in the day of the assembly, saying, Let me not hear again the voice of the LORD my God... that I die not." The witnesses note that the promise answered Israel's plea at Horeb to hear God through a mediator, not directly. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was promised in answer to their request at Horeb, when the law was delivered in so terrible a manner, the tribes assembled at the foot of the mount. The witnesses note that they had begged not to hear God's voice directly, lest they die. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all that thou desiredst — their own request; the in Horeb — terrified by the voice and fire; the let me not hear again — begging a mediator; the God speaking by his Son — the Mediator provided (Heb. 1:2).

The witnesses agree the Prophet was given in gracious answer to the people's own request: at Horeb, terrified by the voice and fire, they had begged to hear God not directly but through a mediator, "let me not hear again the voice of the LORD... that I die not"; God answered that fear with the promise of a Prophet through whom he would speak — and supremely with Christ, the Mediator through whom God speaks safely and savingly to sinners who could not bear his unveiled voice; the dreadful glory of Sinai drove them to seek a mediator, and God provided the true Mediator, "God hath... spoken unto us by his Son" (Heb. 1:2).

So the witnesses make the verse the plea at Horeb: The witnesses note the all that thou desiredst: their own request. The witnesses note the in Horeb: terrified by the voice and fire. The witnesses note the let me not hear again: begging a mediator. The witnesses note the God speaking by his Son: the Mediator provided (Heb. 1:2). The expositors observe the all that thou desiredst: their own request. The expositors observe the in Horeb: terrified by the voice and fire. The expositors observe the let me not hear again: begging a mediator. The expositors observe the God speaking by his Son: the Mediator provided (Heb. 1:2). The believer is taught to mark the all that thou desiredst, the in Horeb, the let me not hear again, the God speaking by his Son, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that the Prophet answered their plea at Horeb to hear God through a mediator — God providing the true Mediator, Christ, through whom he speaks safely to sinners who could not bear his unveiled voice (Heb. 1:2).
17:
"And the LORD said unto me, They have well spoken that which they have spoken." The witnesses note God's approval of the people's request for a mediator. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD said this unto Moses, who carried their request to him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set God's word — they have well spoken. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD said — to Moses their spokesman; the they have well spoken — god approving their request; the the desire for a mediator — a fitting fear of the holy God; the the way of mediation — sinners drawing near through the One appointed (1 Tim. 2:5).

The witnesses agree God owned the people's request as right and good: "they have well spoken," approving their sense of their own unworthiness to hear him directly and their desire for a mediator; the approval shows that the fear which sent them to seek a go-between was not faithless but fitting, the consciousness of sinful creatures who cannot stand before the holy God unveiled; and it commends the way of mediation that God himself had purposed, that sinners draw near to him not in their own persons but through the One he appoints — the request answered with the gift of Christ, the Mediator God himself approved (cf. 1 Tim. 2:5).

So the witnesses make the verse they have well spoken: The witnesses note the the LORD said: to Moses their spokesman. The witnesses note the they have well spoken: God approving their request. The witnesses note the the desire for a mediator: a fitting fear of the holy God. The witnesses note the the way of mediation: sinners drawing near through the One appointed (1 Tim. 2:5). The expositors observe the the LORD said: to Moses their spokesman. The expositors observe the they have well spoken: God approving their request. The expositors observe the the desire for a mediator: a fitting fear of the holy God. The expositors observe the the way of mediation: sinners drawing near through the One appointed (1 Tim. 2:5). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD said, the they have well spoken, the the desire for a mediator, the the way of mediation, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that God approved their request — "they have well spoken" — owning the fitting fear that seeks a mediator, commending the way of mediation answered in Christ, the one Mediator (1 Tim. 2:5).
18:
"I will raise them up a Prophet from among their brethren, like unto thee, and will put my words in his mouth; and he shall speak unto them all that I shall command him." The witnesses note the promise renewed — God would put his words in the mouth of the Prophet, who speaks all God commands. The witnesses note that Gill notes this promise was first made at Sinai, now renewed; the people assured that God would put his words in the Prophet's mouth. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Prophet from among their brethren, like Moses, with God's words in his mouth. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the raise up a Prophet — like unto Moses; the put my words in his mouth — god's message, not his own; the speak all I command — the faithful revealer; the fulfilled in Christ — speaking the Father's words (John 12:49).

The witnesses agree the verse defines the Prophet's office as the perfect organ of God's word: "I will put my words in his mouth, and he shall speak... all that I shall command him" — the Prophet speaking not his own but God's message, the faithful and complete revealer of the divine will; in Christ this is fulfilled beyond all the prophets, for he spake "the words of God" without measure (John 3:34), and said "I have not spoken of myself; but the Father... gave me a commandment, what I should say" (John 12:49) — the Son the very Word of the Father, declaring him fully and finally.

So the witnesses make the verse my words in his mouth: The witnesses note the raise up a Prophet: like unto Moses. The witnesses note the put my words in his mouth: God's message, not his own. The witnesses note the speak all I command: the faithful revealer. The witnesses note the fulfilled in Christ: speaking the Father's words (John 12:49). The expositors observe the raise up a Prophet: like unto Moses. The expositors observe the put my words in his mouth: God's message, not his own. The expositors observe the speak all I command: the faithful revealer. The expositors observe the fulfilled in Christ: speaking the Father's words (John 12:49). The believer is taught to mark the raise up a Prophet, the put my words in his mouth, the speak all I command, the fulfilled in Christ, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that God would put his words in the Prophet's mouth, to speak all he commanded — the perfect organ of God's word, fulfilled in Christ who spoke not of himself but the Father's words (John 12:49).
19:
"And it shall come to pass, that whosoever will not hearken unto my words which he shall speak in my name, I will require it of him." The witnesses note the solemn warning — God will require it of whoever will not hearken to the Prophet. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of whosoever will not hearken to the doctrines of the Gospel, but slights and despises them. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the commandment to hear God's appointed teacher remains in force even to the end of the world. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whosoever will not hearken — refusing the Prophet; the my words in his name — god's own message; the I will require it — god calling to account; the hear at peril of the soul — destroyed who hear not (Acts 3:23).

The witnesses agree the verse arms the Prophet's word with the highest authority and the gravest sanction: to refuse his message is to refuse God's, and "I will require it of him" — God himself will call the despiser to account; Gill applies it to those who slight the gospel of Christ, the great Prophet, and the apostle Peter so applies it, that "every soul, which will not hear that prophet, shall be destroyed from among the people" (Acts 3:23); the warning makes the hearing of Christ a matter of life and death, his word the very word of God, despised at the peril of the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse God will require it: The witnesses note the whosoever will not hearken: refusing the Prophet. The witnesses note the my words in his name: God's own message. The witnesses note the I will require it: God calling to account. The witnesses note the hear at peril of the soul: destroyed who hear not (Acts 3:23). The expositors observe the whosoever will not hearken: refusing the Prophet. The expositors observe the my words in his name: God's own message. The expositors observe the I will require it: God calling to account. The expositors observe the hear at peril of the soul: destroyed who hear not (Acts 3:23). The believer is taught to mark the whosoever will not hearken, the my words in his name, the I will require it, the hear at peril of the soul, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that God will require it of whoever refuses the Prophet's word — to slight him is to slight God, the hearing of Christ a matter of life and death, the despiser destroyed (Acts 3:23).
20:
"But the prophet, which shall presume to speak a word in my name, which I have not commanded him to speak... even that prophet shall die." The witnesses note the false prophet who presumes to speak in God's name, or in the name of other gods, condemned to death. The witnesses note that Gill notes the prophet who presumes to speak in God's name, pretending a mission he never had. The witnesses note that such a prophet, or one speaking in the name of other gods, was to die. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the presume to speak — a word God never commanded; the in my name — falsely claiming his authority; the or other gods — the pretended prophet; the shall die — god guarding the truth of his word (Jer. 23:31).

The witnesses agree the high honour of the true Prophet is guarded by the doom of the false: he who "shall presume to speak" in God's name what God never commanded, or speak in the name of other gods, was to die; the severity protects the people from deception and upholds the sacredness of God's word, that none falsely claim his authority; the lie of a pretended prophet is a grave evil, robbing God's name and leading souls astray, and the law's sentence shows how jealously God guards the truth of his revelation against every counterfeit (cf. Jer. 23:31).

So the witnesses make the verse the false prophet: The witnesses note the presume to speak: a word God never commanded. The witnesses note the in my name: falsely claiming his authority. The witnesses note the or other gods: the pretended prophet. The witnesses note the shall die: God guarding the truth of his word (Jer. 23:31). The expositors observe the presume to speak: a word God never commanded. The expositors observe the in my name: falsely claiming his authority. The expositors observe the or other gods: the pretended prophet. The expositors observe the shall die: God guarding the truth of his word (Jer. 23:31). The believer is taught to mark the presume to speak, the in my name, the or other gods, the shall die, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that the false prophet who presumes to speak in God's name was to die — the honour of the true Prophet guarded by the doom of the false, God jealously protecting the truth of his revelation (Jer. 23:31).
21:
"And if thou say in thine heart, How shall we know the word which the LORD hath not spoken?" The witnesses note the question — how to discern a true word of the LORD from a false. The witnesses note that Gill notes such a thought arises in the mind, a difficulty and query made upon it. The witnesses note that Calvin notes this exception relates to the prohibition already noticed, God having invested his prophets with authority. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if thou say in thine heart — the honest question; the how shall we know — to discern true from false; the the word the LORD hath not spoken — the danger of deception; the a test provided — to try the spirits (1 John 4:1).

The witnesses agree the question is a real and practical one — how the people might distinguish the true prophet from the false: since both might speak "in the name of the LORD," how was the genuine word to be known? the query is honest and necessary, for the people's safety hung on discerning God's true messenger; God does not leave the question unanswered but gives a plain test in the verse following, that his people might not be deceived; the asking and answering teach that God provides his church the means to "try the spirits whether they are of God" (1 John 4:1), and does not require a blind credulity.

So the witnesses make the verse how shall we know: The witnesses note the if thou say in thine heart: the honest question. The witnesses note the how shall we know: to discern true from false. The witnesses note the the word the LORD hath not spoken: the danger of deception. The witnesses note the a test provided: to try the spirits (1 John 4:1). The expositors observe the if thou say in thine heart: the honest question. The expositors observe the how shall we know: to discern true from false. The expositors observe the the word the LORD hath not spoken: the danger of deception. The expositors observe the a test provided: to try the spirits (1 John 4:1). The believer is taught to mark the if thou say in thine heart, the how shall we know, the the word the LORD hath not spoken, the a test provided, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that they might ask how to know a word the LORD had not spoken — the honest question of discernment, God providing a plain test that his people try the spirits and not be deceived (1 John 4:1).
22:
"When a prophet speaketh in the name of the LORD, if the thing follow not, nor come to pass, that is the thing which the LORD hath not spoken... thou shalt not be afraid of him." The witnesses note the test — if a prophet's word does not come to pass, it is not of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes that if the thing does not follow, as the prophecy of Hananiah did not, it is the thing the LORD has not spoken — unless a condition is expressed or implied. The witnesses note that they need not be afraid of such a prophet. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the speaketh in the name of the LORD — the prophet's claim; the if the thing follow not — the prediction failing; the the LORD hath not spoken — the false prophet unmasked; the the true word never fails — the word of God stands for ever (Isa. 40:8).

The witnesses agree the verse gives a plain and sufficient test of the prophet — the fulfilment of his word: if what he foretells "follow not, nor come to pass," he spoke not from God, and need not be feared; Gill rightly notes the exception, that some prophecies are conditional and so wait on the condition, as Jonah's word against Nineveh; yet the rule stands, that a false prediction unmasks a false prophet, the event proving or disproving his claim; the test arms God's people against the pretender and assures them that the true word of the LORD never fails, "the word of our God shall stand for ever" (Isa. 40:8).

So the witnesses make the verse the test of fulfilment: The witnesses note the speaketh in the name of the LORD: the prophet's claim. The witnesses note the if the thing follow not: the prediction failing. The witnesses note the the LORD hath not spoken: the false prophet unmasked. The witnesses note the the true word never fails: the word of God stands for ever (Isa. 40:8). The expositors observe the speaketh in the name of the LORD: the prophet's claim. The expositors observe the if the thing follow not: the prediction failing. The expositors observe the the LORD hath not spoken: the false prophet unmasked. The expositors observe the the true word never fails: the word of God stands for ever (Isa. 40:8). The believer is taught to mark the speaketh in the name of the LORD, the if the thing follow not, the the LORD hath not spoken, the the true word never fails, and to hearken unto the Prophet whom God has raised. The verse thus records that the test of a prophet is the fulfilment of his word — a failed prediction unmasking the false, the true word of the LORD never failing, the word of God standing for ever (Isa. 40:8).
